text: Interval training involves repeated short to long bouts of exercise at intensities equal to or greater than maximal lactate steady-state velocity, interspersed with recovery periods of light exercise or rest.

Outside of the competition season, interval training should be calibrated to velocities associated with physiological responses ranging from maximal lactate steady state to absolute maximal velocity, rather than race-specific paces.
During competition season, runners should use specific velocities from 800m to 5000m race pace to calibrate interval training intensity.
Even world records are not run at a constant pace, so the range of velocities used in a race (not just average pace) should be considered when designing interval training.
Physiological markers such as maximal lactate steady-state velocity provide a more individualized basis for prescribing interval training intensity than competition-derived paces alone.
